skill.name: agent-generation
skill.description: Generate project-aware Claude Code agents using templates, analysis patterns, and examples for specialized coding teams.
skill.summary: Creates focused Claude Code agent templates from project structure, technology stack, architecture, and domain patterns.
skill.author: Cpicon

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but the weak-crypto findings are false positives from markdown words such as description and Design. The remaining risk is medium because the templates grant generated agents Bash, file editing, and web tools, and the analysis guide includes local enumeration commands for codebase inspection.

medium_findings.title: Generated Agents Receive Broad Tool Permissions
medium_findings.description: The core template and reference templates include Bash, Edit, Write, WebFetch, and WebSearch in generated agent tool lists. This is useful for coding agents, but it enables command execution, file modification, and network access when a generated agent is installed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly grant broad tools to generated agents. The context is legitimate developer assistance, so the risk is capability exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Codebase Enumeration Commands In Analysis Guidance
medium_findings.description: The analysis guide includes shell commands that list directories, search source files, and read schema files. These commands support the skill purpose, but they can expose project structure and sensitive model details if used without consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible and perform local codebase discovery. They are ordinary analysis commands, so the security concern depends on the workspace sensitivity.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-crypto alerts match ordinary markdown words such as description, Design, and domain-description. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation, weak hashing, encryption, or password handling logic in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found only descriptive markdown text at the flagged locations. There is no executable crypto code or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Trigger External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts come from markdown fences, inline paths, and documentation examples. The skill does not include scripts or executable source files, but users should still review generated agents before enabling broad tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are markdown documentation and templates. Some fenced bash examples exist, but there is no packaged script that executes them automatically.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Agent Tool Lists
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated agent templates include command execution, file write, task delegation, and web access tools. This pattern should be limited to trusted workspaces and reviewed before publication of generated agents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool lists are explicit and repeated. The pattern is common for coding agents but increases blast radius if generated prompts are misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Reconnaissance Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference guide provides ls, grep, find, and cat commands for discovering project metadata, structure, models, and API routes. These are legitimate analysis commands, not stealth behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly enumerate files and code. Their documented purpose is agent generation, so they are not evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Generic agents miss local project conventions and create inconsistent help. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward focused agents based on real codebase evidence.
actual_capabilities: Defines templates for tech stack, architecture, domain, testing, and DevOps agents.
Maps codebase analysis findings into specialized Claude Code agent roles.
Provides trigger phrase patterns that reduce overlap between generated agents.
Shows examples of project-aware system prompts with file paths and conventions.
Includes guidance for choosing team size from project complexity signals.
limitations: It does not run project analysis by itself.
It assumes Claude Code style agent frontmatter and tool names.
Generated agents need review before use in sensitive repositories.
Template quality depends on accurate project metadata and file examples.

use_cases.title: Create a coding agent team for a product repo
use_cases.description: Generate focused agents for frontend, backend, architecture, and domain questions from one project analysis.

use_cases.title: Document local conventions for new contributors
use_cases.description: Turn directory structure, naming patterns, and key files into agent prompts that explain project-specific practices.

use_cases.title: Specialize AI help for a complex codebase
use_cases.description: Create agents that answer framework, data model, API, testing, and deployment questions using concrete project references.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a starter agent set
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small or medium project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a small Claude Code agent team for my project. Start with tech stack, architecture, and domain agents.

prompt_templates.title: Create a project-aware tech stack agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my project metadata and create a tech stack expert agent. Include frameworks, key directories, conventions, and clear trigger phrases.

prompt_templates.title: Build non-overlapping specialist agents
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a larger team.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate specialist agents with distinct responsibilities. Avoid overlap between architecture, domain, testing, DevOps, and framework expertise.

prompt_templates.title: Audit generated agents before installation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request focused on safety and quality.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these generated agents for excessive tool access, vague triggers, duplicated responsibilities, and missing project evidence.

output_examples.input: Create agents for a Next.js analytics dashboard.
output_examples.output: A recommended team with React, architecture, domain, testing, and DevOps agents, each with clear responsibilities and trigger examples.

output_examples.input: Help new contributors understand where code belongs.
output_examples.output: An architecture expert agent that explains directory layout, module boundaries, naming rules, and placement guidance using project paths.

output_examples.input: Generate a domain expert for billing workflows.
output_examples.output: A domain-focused agent prompt that covers entities, business flows, API routes, validation rules, and key service locations.

best_practices: Use real file paths, versions, and conventions from the target repository.
Keep each generated agent focused on one role with clear trigger conditions.
Review tool permissions before installing generated agents in shared environments.
anti_patterns: Do not create broad agents that answer every coding question.
Do not invent project structure, dependencies, or domain terms.
Do not grant Bash, Write, or web tools when read-only guidance is enough.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It provides templates and guidance for creating project-specific Claude Code agents.

faq.question: Does it analyze my code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides an assistant that already has permission to inspect your repository.

faq.question: Can Codex use the guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex can use the templates and patterns to draft compatible agent instructions.

faq.question: Why are tool permissions a concern?
faq.answer: Generated templates include Bash, Write, and web tools. Review them before use in sensitive workspaces.

faq.question: What projects fit this skill best?
faq.answer: It fits software repositories with enough structure to justify specialized framework, architecture, or domain agents.

faq.question: How should generated agents be reviewed?
faq.answer: Check triggers, project evidence, role boundaries, and tool access before adding agents to a team.
